questions.explanation: TSAs must be trusted by relying parties. Trust is established through: TSA certificates issued by trusted CAs, accreditation by national bodies (CCA in India, ETSI in Europe), or being listed in trusted TSA root stores.
questions.takeaway: TSA trust = TSA cert from trusted CA + national accreditation (CCA India, ETSI Europe)

questions.question: In Indian PKI, what classes of digital signature certificates exist?
options: Class 1 (email validation only), Class 2 (deprecated 2021), Class 3 (highest, in-person verification)
questions.explanation: Indian DSC classes: Class 1 (basic, email validation, low trust), Class 2 (identity from trusted database, now deprecated by CCA in 2021), Class 3 (highest assurance, in-person verification, required for e-tendering).
questions.takeaway: India DSC: Class 1 (email only), Class 2 (deprecated 2021), Class 3 (highest, in-person)

questions.question: What is 'e-Mudhra' in India?
options: A licensed Certifying Authority in India (under CCA) providing digital signature certificates and PKI services
questions.explanation: eMudhra is one of India's CCA-licensed Certifying Authorities providing digital signature certificates (Class 3), OCSP services, and trust services. It is among the largest CAs in India.
questions.takeaway: eMudhra = CCA-licensed Indian Certifying Authority (Class 3 DSC, OCSP services)

questions.question: What is PKCS#3?
options: Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement Standard — defines DH key exchange parameters and protocol
questions.explanation: PKCS#3 defines the Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement Standard, specifying the format for DH parameters (prime p, generator g) and the DH key agreement protocol used for symmetric key establishment.
questions.takeaway: PKCS#3 = Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement Standard (defines DH parameters format)

questions.question: What is PKCS#9?
options: Defines selected object classes and attribute types for use in PKCS#6 (extended certs), PKCS#7 (CMS), PKCS#8, PKCS#10
questions.explanation: PKCS#9 defines selected attribute types and object classes used in: certificate extensions, signed data (S/MIME), private key info (PKCS#8), and certificate requests (PKCS#10). Includes email address, challenge password attributes.
questions.takeaway: PKCS#9 = attribute types for certificates and CSRs (email, challenge password, etc.)

questions.question: What is PKCS#15?
options: Cryptographic Token Information Format Standard — defines a way to store cryptographic information on cryptographic tokens (smartcards)
questions.explanation: PKCS#15 (CTIFS) defines a standard format for storing cryptographic information (certificates, keys) on tokens like smartcards and USB keys, enabling interoperability between different token implementations.
questions.takeaway: PKCS#15 = Cryptographic Token Information Format (smartcard/USB key interoperability)

questions.question: What is a 'CMVP' (Cryptographic Module Validation Program)?
options: NIST and CCCS program that validates cryptographic modules against FIPS 140-2/140-3 standards
questions.explanation: The CMVP (run jointly by NIST and the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security) validates cryptographic modules against FIPS 140-2/140-3 standards. Validated modules are listed on the NIST website and required for US government use.
questions.takeaway: CMVP = NIST program validating crypto modules against FIPS 140-2/140-3

questions.question: What does FIPS 140-2 Level 4 require?
options: Complete physical security envelope — detects and responds to physical penetration attempts from any direction
questions.explanation: FIPS 140-2 Level 4 requires a complete physical security envelope detecting penetration attempts from any direction and zeroizing all plaintext critical security parameters in response. Highest level for extreme security environments.
questions.takeaway: FIPS 140-2 L4 = complete physical envelope + auto-zeroize on any penetration attempt

questions.question: What are the FIPS 140-2 security levels in order?
options: Level 1 (software requirements), Level 2 (tamper evidence), Level 3 (tamper resistance + identity auth), Level 4 (full physical envelope)
questions.explanation: FIPS 140-2 has 4 levels: L1=basic algorithm requirements (software OK), L2=tamper evidence (seals, coatings), L3=tamper resistance (hard enclosure + identity auth + zeroization), L4=complete physical envelope.
questions.takeaway: FIPS 140-2: L1=software OK, L2=tamper evidence, L3=tamper resist, L4=full envelope

questions.question: What is 'Common Criteria' (CC) in security evaluation?
options: A common set of network rules
An international standard (ISO/IEC 15408) for evaluating IT security products through defined evaluation assurance levels (EAL1-7)
questions.explanation: Common Criteria (ISO/IEC 15408) provides a framework for evaluating IT security product claims. EAL (Evaluation Assurance Level) ranges from EAL1 (functionally tested) to EAL7 (formally verified design). Used for government procurements.
questions.takeaway: Common Criteria = ISO/IEC 15408 security evaluation standard (EAL1-EAL7 assurance levels)

questions.question: What is OTP (One-Time Password) and how does it improve security?
options: A password generated from OTP algorithm only
A password valid for only one login session, preventing credential reuse attacks even if intercepted
questions.explanation: OTPs are valid for a single transaction/login. Even if intercepted (man-in-the-middle, phishing, keylogging), an OTP cannot be reused. Types: TOTP (time-based), HOTP (counter-based), SMS OTP.
questions.takeaway: OTP = one-time use password (invalid after use; prevents replay of captured credentials)

questions.question: What is HOTP (HMAC-based One-Time Password)?
options: An OTP algorithm using HMAC-SHA1 with a counter that increments with each authentication (RFC 4226)
questions.explanation: HOTP (RFC 4226) generates OTPs using HMAC-SHA1(K, counter) where K is a shared secret and counter increments with each use. The server and client keep synchronized counters. Used in RSA SecurID tokens.
questions.takeaway: HOTP (RFC 4226) = HMAC-SHA1(secret, counter) — counter increments each use

questions.question: What is the difference between TOTP and HOTP?
options: TOTP uses current time (30-sec windows, RFC 6238); HOTP uses incrementing counter (RFC 4226)
TOTP is more secure than HOTP always
TOTP uses hardware; HOTP uses software
questions.explanation: TOTP (RFC 6238) generates OTPs from current time (30-second windows). HOTP (RFC 4226) uses a counter incrementing per use. TOTP is synchronization-free (no counter drift) but requires accurate clocks.
questions.takeaway: TOTP = time-based (30s windows, RFC 6238); HOTP = counter-based (RFC 4226)

questions.question: What is a 'push notification' MFA method?
options: An MFA method sending a login approval request to the user's mobile app; user approves/denies on their phone
questions.explanation: Push notification MFA (used by Duo, Microsoft Authenticator) sends an authentication request to a registered mobile app. The user approves or denies the request on their phone, combining 'something you have' authentication.
questions.takeaway: Push MFA = login approval sent to phone app (Duo, Microsoft Authenticator)

questions.question: What is 'step-up authentication'?
options: Requiring additional authentication factors when accessing higher-risk resources or sensitive operations
questions.explanation: Step-up authentication triggers additional authentication factors when a user attempts to access more sensitive resources (e.g., already logged in with password, but viewing financial data requires MFA).
questions.takeaway: Step-up auth = additional factors triggered for higher-risk actions/resources

questions.question: What is the 'authentication assurance level' (AAL) in NIST SP 800-63B?
options: NIST standard defining 3 authentication assurance levels (AAL1, AAL2, AAL3) with increasing strength requirements
questions.explanation: NIST SP 800-63B defines: AAL1 (single factor; password OK), AAL2 (MFA required; TOTP or cryptographic device), AAL3 (hardware cryptographic device required; phishing resistant; e.g., FIDO2 security key).
questions.takeaway: NIST AAL: AAL1=password, AAL2=MFA+TOTP, AAL3=hardware crypto key (FIDO2)

questions.question: What is 'contextual authentication' (risk-based authentication)?
options: Authentication that considers context (location, device, time, behavior) to assess risk and require additional factors if suspicious
questions.explanation: Risk-based/contextual authentication evaluates login context: location (new country?), device (known?), time (unusual hour?), behavior (unusual pattern?). High-risk contexts trigger step-up authentication.
questions.takeaway: Risk-based auth = context (location, device, time) determines required auth strength

questions.question: What is 'knowledge-based authentication' (KBA)?
options: Authentication using security questions based on personal information (mother's maiden name, first car)
questions.explanation: KBA uses security questions with personal answers (mother's maiden name, first pet, etc.) as authentication. Static KBA is considered weak because answers can be found on social media. Dynamic KBA uses credit history.
questions.takeaway: KBA = security questions authentication (weak due to social media exposure)

questions.question: What is a 'smart card' in authentication?
options: An ISO 7816 card containing a secure processor and certificate/key used for PKI authentication and digital signing
A credit card with chip for payments only
questions.explanation: A smart card is a pocket-sized card with an embedded secure microprocessor (ISO 7816). It stores private keys, certificates, and can perform PKI operations (signing, decryption) directly on the card without exposing the key.
questions.takeaway: Smart card = ISO 7816 card with secure processor (stores private key; performs crypto on-card)

questions.question: What is 'PIV' (Personal Identity Verification) card?
options: A US government smart card standard (FIPS 201) for physical and logical access using PKI certificates
questions.explanation: PIV (FIPS 201) is the US government standard for smart cards used for physical access to buildings and logical access to networks. PIV cards contain PKI certificates for authentication, digital signing, and encryption.
questions.takeaway: PIV (FIPS 201) = US gov't smart card for physical + logical access (PKI-based)

questions.question: What is 'SAML 2.0' and what problem does it solve?
options: An XML-based SSO protocol solving cross-domain authentication — users log in once at IdP to access multiple SPs
questions.explanation: SAML 2.0 enables SSO across different organizations. The Identity Provider (IdP) authenticates the user and issues XML assertions to Service Providers (SPs). No credentials are shared — only authentication assertions.
questions.takeaway: SAML 2.0 = XML SSO (IdP authenticates → issues XML assertion → SPs trust it)

questions.question: What is 'JWT' (JSON Web Token) and where is it used?
options: A compact, URL-safe token (Header.Payload.Signature) used for stateless authentication and information exchange in APIs
questions.explanation: JWT is a compact token in three parts: Header (algorithm), Payload (claims/data), Signature (HMAC-SHA256 or RS256). Used for stateless auth in REST APIs, OAuth 2.0 access tokens, and OpenID Connect ID tokens.
questions.takeaway: JWT = Header.Payload.Signature (base64); stateless auth for APIs and OAuth/OIDC

questions.question: What is the 'OAuth 2.0 implicit flow' and why is it deprecated?
options: A deprecated flow sending access tokens directly to browser redirect URI (security risk: token in URL)
questions.explanation: The implicit flow returned access tokens directly in the redirect URI fragment (visible in browser history, referrer headers). Deprecated in OAuth 2.1 because of token leakage risks. Replaced by PKCE authorization code flow.
questions.takeaway: OAuth implicit flow = deprecated (token in URL = security risk); use PKCE code flow instead

questions.question: What is 'OpenID Connect' (OIDC) scope 'openid'?
options: A required scope in OIDC that triggers identity token (ID token) issuance alongside the access token
questions.explanation: In OIDC, the 'openid' scope is mandatory and signals to the Authorization Server to issue an ID Token (JWT containing user identity claims like sub, name, email) in addition to the access token.
questions.takeaway: OIDC 'openid' scope = required; triggers ID Token (JWT with user identity claims)

questions.question: What is 'FIDO UAF' (Universal Authentication Framework)?
options: FIDO standard for passwordless authentication on mobile devices using local biometrics (fingerprint, face)
questions.explanation: FIDO UAF enables passwordless authentication on mobile devices. Users register using local biometrics (fingerprint, face ID). During authentication, the biometric unlocks the private key, which signs a server challenge.
questions.takeaway: FIDO UAF = passwordless mobile auth using biometrics (fingerprint/face unlocks private key)

questions.question: What is 'FIDO U2F' (Universal 2nd Factor)?
options: FIDO standard for adding strong 2nd factor authentication using hardware security keys (YubiKey) to existing password systems
questions.explanation: FIDO U2F adds strong hardware-based 2nd factor to password authentication. The hardware key signs a challenge from the server using a device-bound private key. The challenge includes the origin URL, preventing phishing.
questions.takeaway: FIDO U2F = hardware key as strong 2FA (phishing-resistant; origin-bound challenge)

questions.question: Why is FIDO2/WebAuthn phishing-resistant?
options: The authenticator includes the relying party's origin in the signed challenge; a fake site has a different origin, making stolen responses useless
questions.explanation: WebAuthn signs the challenge along with the relying party ID (rpId = the actual website origin). If a user is phished to evil.com instead of bank.com, the signed assertion contains evil.com's rpId which the real bank server will reject.
questions.takeaway: FIDO2 phishing resistant = origin (rpId) bound to signature; fake site's response rejected by real server

questions.question: What is 'Zero Trust' in the context of API security?
options: Every API call requires authentication and authorization, regardless of network location; no implicit trust for internal APIs
questions.explanation: Zero Trust for APIs: Every API call is authenticated (via JWT, OAuth token, mTLS client cert) regardless of whether it comes from inside or outside the network perimeter. No implicit trust for internal services.
questions.takeaway: Zero Trust APIs = every call authenticated (JWT/OAuth/mTLS); no implicit trust for internal

questions.question: What is 'mTLS' (mutual TLS)?
options: TLS where both client and server authenticate each other using certificates (bidirectional certificate authentication)
questions.explanation: Mutual TLS (mTLS) requires both the TLS server AND client to present valid X.509 certificates. Used in microservices security (Zero Trust), API authentication, and IoT device authentication.
questions.takeaway: mTLS = both client AND server present certificates (bidirectional authentication)

questions.question: What is 'BeyondCorp' and how does it relate to Zero Trust?
options: Google's Zero Trust network architecture where access is based on device/user identity and context, not network location
questions.explanation: BeyondCorp is Google's implementation of Zero Trust principles — employees access resources from any network (including public internet) based on device compliance and user identity, eliminating reliance on VPN/network perimeter.
questions.takeaway: BeyondCorp = Google's Zero Trust impl (identity+device-based access, no VPN perimeter)

questions.question: What TLS version should be configured as minimum in 2024?
questions.explanation: TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are deprecated (RFC 8996, 2021) and should not be used. Minimum recommended is TLS 1.2. TLS 1.3 is preferred for its improved security and performance. SSL 2.0/3.0 were deprecated years ago.
questions.takeaway: Minimum TLS in 2024 = TLS 1.2 (TLS 1.3 preferred); TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecated (RFC 8996)

questions.question: What is 'HTTP Strict Transport Security' (HSTS) and how does it work?
options: A response header (Strict-Transport-Security) instructing browsers to always use HTTPS for the domain, with optional preloading
questions.explanation: HSTS (RFC 6797): server sends 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains' header. Browsers store this and always use HTTPS for that domain (even for http:// URLs) for the max-age duration.
questions.takeaway: HSTS = HTTP header telling browser: always use HTTPS (max-age, includeSubDomains, preload)

questions.question: What is the 'HSTS preload list'?
options: A list of domains hardcoded into browsers as HTTPS-only, protecting them from HSTS bypass on first visit
questions.explanation: The HSTS preload list is a list of domains hardcoded into browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) as always HTTPS-only. Domains submit to hstspreload.org. Protects from MiTM on first visit (before browser receives HSTS header).
questions.takeaway: HSTS preload = domains hardcoded in browsers as HTTPS-only (protection from day 1 visit)

questions.question: What is 'TLS 1.3 0-RTT resumption' and its security concern?
options: Allows resumed sessions to send application data before server handshake completes (faster) but risks replay attacks
questions.explanation: TLS 1.3 0-RTT (Zero Round Trip Time Resumption) allows resuming sessions with no handshake delay using PSK. Risk: early application data (0-RTT data) can be replayed by attackers. Should be used only for idempotent requests.
questions.takeaway: TLS 1.3 0-RTT = faster resumption but replay attack risk (use only for idempotent GET requests)

questions.question: What is an 'S/MIME email signature' vs 'S/MIME email encryption'?
options: Signature = sender's private key signs (authentication/integrity); Encryption = recipient's public key encrypts (confidentiality)
questions.explanation: S/MIME signature: sender signs with their private key (recipient verifies with sender's public key) → provides authentication and integrity. S/MIME encryption: sender encrypts with recipient's public key → provides confidentiality.
questions.takeaway: S/MIME sign = sender's private key; S/MIME encrypt = recipient's public key

questions.question: What is 'PGP key server' used for?
options: A publicly accessible directory where PGP users can upload and search for other users' public keys (e.g., keys.openpgp.org)
questions.explanation: PGP key servers (keys.openpgp.org, pgp.mit.edu) allow users to publish their public keys and find others' public keys. Unlike PKI, PGP key servers don't validate identity — they just distribute keys.
questions.takeaway: PGP key server = public directory for PGP public keys (no identity validation, just distribution)

questions.question: What is 'WKD' (Web Key Directory) for PGP?
options: A simple protocol for publishing PGP keys via HTTPS on the key owner's own domain (e.g., openpgpkey.example.com)
questions.explanation: WKD (Web Key Directory) allows users to publish PGP public keys on their own web server under a well-known URL. Email clients can auto-fetch recipients' keys based on their email domain.
questions.takeaway: WKD = PGP key discovery via HTTPS on own domain (auto-fetch by email domain)

questions.question: What ports does POP3S, IMAPS, and SMTPS use?
questions.explanation: Secure email ports: POP3S (TLS) = 995, IMAPS (TLS) = 993, SMTPS (implicit TLS) = 465, SMTP with STARTTLS (explicit TLS) = 587. Plain text: POP3=110, IMAP=143, SMTP=25.
questions.takeaway: Secure email: POP3S=995, IMAPS=993, SMTPS=465, SMTP+STARTTLS=587

questions.question: What is 'STARTTLS' in email security?
options: An email protocol extension upgrading a plain-text connection to TLS (opportunistic encryption via SMTP port 587)
questions.explanation: STARTTLS upgrades a plaintext SMTP/IMAP/POP3 connection to TLS after the initial handshake. SMTP port 587 uses STARTTLS for submission. Unlike port 465 (implicit TLS), STARTTLS starts as plaintext.
questions.takeaway: STARTTLS = upgrades plaintext connection to TLS (SMTP port 587); vs implicit TLS (port 465)

questions.question: What is DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)?
options: An email authentication method using RSA/ECC digital signatures to verify email originates from the claimed domain
questions.explanation: DKIM allows the sending mail server to sign emails with a private key. The corresponding public key is published in DNS (TXT record). Receiving servers verify the DKIM signature, confirming the email came from the domain.
questions.takeaway: DKIM = email digital signature (private key signs; public key in DNS TXT record)

questions.question: What is 'SPF' (Sender Policy Framework)?
options: A DNS-based email authentication listing authorized mail servers for a domain (prevents email spoofing)
questions.explanation: SPF is a DNS TXT record listing which IP addresses/servers are authorized to send email from a domain. Receiving servers check SPF to detect email spoofing — if the sending IP isn't in the SPF record, it may be spam/spoofed.
questions.takeaway: SPF = DNS record listing authorized mail servers (prevents email spoofing)

questions.question: What is 'DMARC' (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance)?
options: A policy framework building on SPF and DKIM to specify how email authentication failures should be handled (quarantine/reject)
questions.explanation: DMARC builds on SPF and DKIM: it specifies what to do with emails that fail authentication (none, quarantine, reject) and provides reporting. DMARC requires SPF and/or DKIM alignment with the From: domain.
questions.takeaway: DMARC = policy for SPF/DKIM failures (none/quarantine/reject) + reporting

questions.question: What is 'Section 43A' of India's IT Act?
options: Requires body corporates handling sensitive personal data to implement reasonable security practices or pay compensation
questions.explanation: Section 43A of IT Act (added 2008 amendment) requires any company possessing sensitive personal data to implement and maintain 'reasonable security practices'. Failure causing wrongful loss can result in compensation claims.
questions.takeaway: IT Act Section 43A = data protection requirement; companies must secure sensitive personal data

questions.question: What are 'Sensitive Personal Data or Information' (SPDI) under IT Act India?
options: Passwords, financial information, health data, biometric data, sexual orientation — defined in IT (Reasonable Security Practices) Rules 2011
questions.explanation: Under IT Rules 2011 (under Section 43A), SPDI includes: passwords, financial info (bank/card details), health/medical records, sexual orientation, biometric data. More stringent protection required for SPDI.
questions.takeaway: SPDI India = passwords, financial info, health, biometric, sexual orientation (IT Rules 2011)

questions.question: What is 'PDPB' / 'DPDP Act 2023' in India?
options: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 — India's comprehensive personal data protection law
questions.explanation: India's DPDP Act 2023 (Digital Personal Data Protection Act) is India's comprehensive data privacy law. It defines data principals, data fiduciaries, consent requirements, and data protection obligations similar to GDPR.
questions.takeaway: DPDP Act 2023 = India's data privacy law (similar to GDPR; consent, purpose limitation)

questions.question: What is 'blockchain consensus mechanism'?
options: The method by which distributed nodes agree on which transactions are valid (PoW, PoS, DPoS, PBFT)
Block verification by a central server
questions.explanation: Blockchain consensus mechanisms allow distributed nodes to agree on the correct state of the blockchain without a central authority. Proof of Work (Bitcoin) uses computational puzzles; Proof of Stake uses validator deposits.
questions.takeaway: Blockchain consensus = distributed agreement (PoW=computational puzzles, PoS=validator stakes)

questions.question: What is 'Proof of Work' (PoW) in blockchain?
options: Miners compete to solve computationally expensive puzzles (SHA-256 in Bitcoin) to add blocks and earn rewards
questions.explanation: PoW (Bitcoin, Ethereum pre-merge) requires miners to find a nonce such that SHA-256(SHA-256(block_header)) produces a hash below a target difficulty. This expensive computation prevents Sybil attacks and secures the network.
questions.takeaway: PoW = miners find nonce where hash < target (Bitcoin uses SHA-256; energy intensive)

questions.question: What is 'digital asset' in the context of blockchain and PKI?
options: A cryptographic token (cryptocurrency, NFT, tokenized asset) whose ownership is proven via private key possession on a blockchain
questions.explanation: Digital assets (cryptocurrencies, NFTs, DeFi tokens) use PKI: ownership is proven by possessing the private key corresponding to the blockchain address. Transferring assets requires signing transactions with the private key.
questions.takeaway: Digital asset ownership = possession of private key (sign transaction to transfer)

questions.question: What is an Ethereum 'wallet' in cryptography terms?
options: A software/hardware tool managing private keys (and derived Ethereum addresses) for signing blockchain transactions
questions.explanation: An Ethereum wallet manages private keys and computes the corresponding Ethereum address (last 20 bytes of Keccak-256 hash of public key). The wallet signs transactions with the private key — it doesn't store ETH itself.
questions.takeaway: Crypto wallet = manages private keys (address = Keccak256(public_key)[-20 bytes])

questions.question: What is 'LDAP Distinguished Name' (DN) structure?
options: A hierarchical naming structure (cn=user, ou=dept, dc=domain, dc=com) identifying entries in an LDAP directory
questions.explanation: LDAP DN example: 'cn=John Smith, ou=IT, dc=example, dc=com' — cn=Common Name (person), ou=Organizational Unit, dc=Domain Component. The DN uniquely identifies an entry in the LDAP directory tree.
questions.takeaway: LDAP DN = cn=John, ou=IT, dc=example, dc=com (hierarchical unique identifier)

questions.question: What is 'LDAPS' and why is it needed?
options: LDAP over SSL/TLS (port 636) encrypting directory queries including credentials — required since plain LDAP sends passwords in cleartext
questions.explanation: Regular LDAP (port 389) sends all data including bind (login) credentials in plaintext. LDAPS (port 636) wraps LDAP in TLS, encrypting all communications. Required for secure directory authentication.
questions.takeaway: LDAPS (port 636) = LDAP encrypted with TLS (necessary since LDAP port 389 sends passwords in cleartext)

questions.question: What is 'PKI certificate lifecycle' and what phases does it include?
options: Enrollment, issuance, distribution, use, renewal, revocation, and archival/audit
questions.explanation: PKI certificate lifecycle phases: 1) Enrollment (request/CSR), 2) Issuance (CA signing), 3) Distribution (delivery to subject), 4) Use (authentication, signing, encryption), 5) Renewal (before expiry), 6) Revocation (if compromised), 7) Archival.
questions.takeaway: Cert lifecycle = Enroll → Issue → Distribute → Use → Renew → Revoke → Archive

questions.question: What is the difference between 'authentication' and 'identification'?
options: Identification = claiming an identity (presenting username); Authentication = proving the claimed identity (password, biometric)
questions.explanation: Identification is presenting an identity claim (username, user ID). Authentication is proving that claim is genuine (password, biometric, certificate). You identify first, then authenticate.
questions.takeaway: Identification = claim identity (username); Authentication = PROVE it (password, biometric)

questions.question: What is 'non-repudiation' and which security service provides it?
options: The inability to deny an action — digital signatures provide non-repudiation since only the private key holder could have signed
questions.explanation: Non-repudiation ensures a party cannot deny having performed an action. Digital signatures provide non-repudiation: only the holder of the private key could produce the signature, making denial impossible.
questions.takeaway: Non-repudiation = cannot deny action; digital signatures provide this (private key binds identity)

questions.question: What is 'PKIX' path validation algorithm?
options: The algorithm (RFC 5280) for validating X.509 certificate chains: verifying signatures, validity periods, revocation, and constraints at each step
questions.explanation: PKIX (RFC 5280) path validation processes a chain of certificates from end-entity to trust anchor: 1) Verify each cert's signature, 2) Check validity period, 3) Check revocation (CRL/OCSP), 4) Verify name constraints and policy constraints.
questions.takeaway: PKIX path validation = verify each cert: signature + validity + revocation + constraints

questions.question: What is 'certificate pinning bypass' technique?
options: Techniques like using Frida/SSL Kill Switch to hook system SSL functions on mobile devices to bypass certificate pinning checks
questions.explanation: Attackers/pentesters bypass pinning using: Frida (runtime code injection), SSL Kill Switch (iOS jailbreak tweak), SSLUnpinning (Android), or repackaging the app with pinning removed. Forces apps to accept proxied traffic.
questions.takeaway: Pinning bypass = Frida/SSLKillSwitch hooks SSL functions on mobile (pentest/attack technique)

questions.question: What is 'transparency' in cryptographic systems?
options: Using open, publicly auditable algorithms and implementations rather than proprietary 'security through obscurity'
questions.explanation: Cryptographic transparency means using publicly known, peer-reviewed algorithms (open standards like AES, SHA-3, RSA) rather than secret proprietary ones. Open algorithms can be widely analyzed for weaknesses.
questions.takeaway: Cryptographic transparency = open standards + public audit (vs security through obscurity)

questions.question: What is 'quantum key distribution' (QKD)?
options: Using quantum mechanics (photon polarization) to securely distribute encryption keys — any eavesdropping disturbs the quantum state and is detectable
questions.explanation: QKD uses quantum mechanics (photon polarization in BB84 protocol) for physically secure key distribution. Any eavesdropping disturbs the quantum state, making interception detectable. Provides information-theoretic security.
questions.takeaway: QKD = quantum physics key distribution (eavesdropping detectable; BB84 protocol)

questions.question: What is the 'BB84' protocol?
options: The first quantum key distribution protocol (Bennett and Brassard 1984) using photon polarization to securely exchange keys
questions.explanation: BB84 (Bennett-Brassard 1984) is the first QKD protocol. It uses two bases for photon polarization to transmit key bits. Any eavesdropping introduces detectable errors in the quantum channel.
questions.takeaway: BB84 = first QKD protocol (1984); photon polarization; eavesdropping creates detectable errors

questions.question: What are 'post-quantum cryptography' algorithms standardized by NIST in 2024?
questions.explanation: NIST PQC standards (2024): ML-KEM (formerly Kyber) for key encapsulation, ML-DSA (formerly Dilithium) and FALCON for digital signatures, SLH-DSA (formerly SPHINCS+) for hash-based signatures. Resistant to quantum attacks.
questions.takeaway: NIST PQC 2024: ML-KEM (Kyber), ML-DSA (Dilithium), FALCON, SLH-DSA (SPHINCS+)

questions.question: What is 'Shor's algorithm' and why does it threaten current cryptography?
options: A quantum algorithm that can factor large integers and solve discrete logarithms in polynomial time, breaking RSA and ECC
An algorithm for fast file sorting
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm (1994) can factor n-bit integers in O(n^3) time on a quantum computer, breaking RSA. It also solves DLP (breaking ECC, DH). On a sufficiently large quantum computer, all current PKI would be vulnerable.
questions.takeaway: Shor's algorithm = quantum factoring/DLP in polynomial time (breaks RSA, ECC, DH)

questions.question: What is 'Grover's algorithm' and how does it affect symmetric encryption?
options: A quantum algorithm providing quadratic speedup for searching, effectively halving symmetric key strength (AES-256 → 128-bit security)
A database search algorithm with no crypto impact
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides O(√N) quantum speedup for unstructured search. Applied to symmetric key brute force: AES-128 provides only 64-bit quantum security. Solution: use AES-256 (128-bit quantum security).
questions.takeaway: Grover's algorithm = halves symmetric key security (AES-128=64-bit quantum; use AES-256)

questions.question: What is 'certificate transparency' Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT)?
options: A promise from a CT log that a certificate will be logged; included in TLS certificates or TLS extension for browser verification
questions.explanation: An SCT is a signed promise from a CT log operator that a certificate has been submitted for inclusion. Browsers require SCTs (embedded in cert, TLS extension, or OCSP staple) to verify CT compliance for EV and DV certs.
questions.takeaway: SCT = CT log's signed promise cert was submitted; browsers require it for trust

questions.question: What is a 'certificate' in PGP vs X.509?
options: X.509 = CA-signed hierarchical cert (structured format); PGP = user-signed certificate (web of trust, flexible)
questions.explanation: X.509 certificates are issued and signed by CAs in a strict hierarchy (structured ASN.1 format). PGP certificates are key packets with multiple signatures from various users — they use the web of trust model, not hierarchical CA.
questions.takeaway: X.509 cert = CA-signed, hierarchical, ASN.1 format; PGP cert = user-signed, web of trust

questions.question: What is 'PKCS#10' (Certificate Signing Request format)?
options: The standard format for Certificate Signing Requests containing applicant's public key, DN, optional attributes, and self-signature
questions.explanation: PKCS#10 defines the CSR format: 1) CertificationRequestInfo (subject DN + public key + optional attributes), 2) Signature algorithm, 3) Self-signature (proves key ownership). Sent to CA for certificate issuance.
questions.takeaway: PKCS#10 = CSR format: DN + public key + optional attributes + self-signature

questions.question: What is 'OCSP nonce'?
options: A random value in OCSP requests preventing replay of cached OCSP responses
questions.explanation: An OCSP nonce is a random value included in the OCSP request. The OCSP responder must include the same nonce in its response. This prevents replay attacks where old OCSP responses are served.
questions.takeaway: OCSP nonce = random value preventing replay of cached OCSP responses

questions.question: What is a 'certification path' and why is it important?
options: The ordered chain of certificates from end-entity to trust anchor needed to establish trust — browsers validate this path
questions.explanation: A certification path (certificate chain) is the sequence of certificates starting from the end-entity certificate through intermediate CAs up to a trusted Root CA. Path validation ensures each certificate is valid and properly signed.
questions.takeaway: Certification path = ordered chain from end-entity → Intermediate CA → Root CA (trust path)

questions.question: What is 'certificate transparency log' and what is it built on?
options: An append-only, publicly auditable Merkle tree-based log of all submitted TLS certificates
questions.explanation: CT logs are built on Merkle trees (hash trees) providing: append-only property (can't delete entries), verifiable consistency (tree heads can be compared), and inclusion proofs (prove a cert is in the log).
questions.takeaway: CT log = append-only Merkle hash tree (tamper-evident; consistency and inclusion proofs)

questions.question: What is the difference between 'TLS inspection' and 'TLS interception'?
options: Both decrypt TLS traffic; inspection = for security monitoring (corporate proxy); interception = unauthorized MiTM attack
TLS inspection is legal; interception is not always
questions.explanation: TLS inspection (corporate proxy, firewall): authorized MiTM using a trusted internal CA certificate to decrypt, scan, and re-encrypt traffic for security monitoring. TLS interception by attackers: unauthorized, malicious MiTM.
questions.takeaway: TLS inspection = authorized corporate MiTM (internal CA); TLS interception = unauthorized attack

questions.question: What is 'PKCS#11 token' initialization?
options: The process of setting up a cryptographic token (smartcard/HSM) with a Security Officer PIN, User PIN, and label before use
questions.explanation: PKCS#11 token initialization sets up the token for use: 1) Set Token Label, 2) Set SO PIN (Security Officer - admin), 3) Initialize User PIN. After init, objects (keys, certificates) can be created using the User PIN.
questions.takeaway: PKCS#11 init = set label + SO PIN (admin) + User PIN (user) before use

questions.question: What is 'key ceremony' and who participates?
options: Formal ceremony for Root CA key generation involving multiple trusted individuals (key custodians), HSMs, auditors, and video recording
questions.explanation: A CA key ceremony is a formal, controlled process for generating Root CA keys with: HSM (key generation), multiple key custodians (M-of-N sharing), independent auditors, video recording, script, and secure facility. Ensures no single point of trust.
questions.takeaway: Key ceremony = formal Root CA key generation with HSM + M-of-N custodians + auditors + recording

questions.question: What is 'certificate enrollment protocol' SCEP used for?
options: Secure Certificate Exchange Protocol for websites
Simple Certificate Enrollment Protocol (RFC 8894) for automated certificate enrollment of network devices (routers, switches, firewalls)
questions.explanation: SCEP (Simple Certificate Enrollment Protocol, RFC 8894) was originally designed by Cisco for enrolling network equipment (routers, switches) with certificates automatically using HTTP with DES/AES encryption.
questions.takeaway: SCEP = Simple Certificate Enrollment Protocol for network device (router/switch) auto-enrollment

questions.question: What is 'certificate template' in Microsoft ADCS?
options: A configurable blueprint in Windows ADCS defining certificate properties (key usage, validity, CSP) for different certificate types
questions.explanation: ADCS certificate templates define the blueprint for certificates: key usage, EKU, cryptographic providers allowed, validity period, subject requirements, and enrollment permissions. Organizations customize templates for specific use cases.
questions.takeaway: ADCS cert template = blueprint for cert type (key usage, EKU, validity, enrollment permissions)

questions.question: What is the difference between 'DER' and 'PEM' certificate formats?
options: DER is for Linux; PEM is for Windows
DER = binary (encoded in ASN.1 DER format); PEM = Base64-encoded DER with header/footer lines (-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----)
DER contains private key; PEM contains public key only
questions.explanation: DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is binary ASN.1 encoding used on Windows (.cer, .der). PEM is Base64-encoded DER with header/footer (-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----) used in Linux/OpenSSL. Both contain the same certificate data.
questions.takeaway: DER = binary ASN.1 (Windows .cer); PEM = Base64 DER with headers (Linux/OpenSSL)

questions.question: What OpenSSL command converts PEM to DER format?
questions.explanation: 'openssl x509 -in cert.pem -outform DER -out cert.der' converts a PEM certificate to DER binary format. The reverse: 'openssl x509 -in cert.der -inform DER -out cert.pem' converts DER to PEM.
questions.takeaway: PEM to DER = 'openssl x509 -in cert.pem -outform DER -out cert.der'

questions.question: What is 'CMP' (Certificate Management Protocol)?
options: IETF protocol (RFC 4210) for managing certificates: enrollment, revocation, key update, and cross-certification between CAs
questions.explanation: CMP (RFC 4210) is a comprehensive PKI management protocol for: initial registration, certificate request/response, key update, revocation, cross-certification. Used in telecom PKI, machine/IoT cert management.
questions.takeaway: CMP (RFC 4210) = comprehensive PKI management protocol (enroll, revoke, key update, cross-certify)

questions.question: What is 'EST' (Enrollment over Secure Transport)?
options: RFC 7030 protocol providing certificate enrollment services over HTTPS — a modern replacement for SCEP
questions.explanation: EST (Enrollment over Secure Transport, RFC 7030) provides certificate enrollment using HTTPS (TLS with CA cert validation). It is simpler and more secure than SCEP, using standard HTTP with JSON/PKCS#10 format.
questions.takeaway: EST (RFC 7030) = modern certificate enrollment over HTTPS (replaces SCEP)

questions.question: What is 'certificate rotation' in DevOps/microservices?
options: Automated process of replacing expiring certificates in production without downtime (short-lived certs, automated via Vault/cert-manager)
questions.explanation: Certificate rotation in DevOps automates replacing expiring certificates in production microservices. Tools: HashiCorp Vault (cert engine), Kubernetes cert-manager, AWS ACM. Short-lived certs (hours/days) require frequent automated rotation.
questions.takeaway: Cert rotation in DevOps = automated cert replacement (Vault, cert-manager; short-lived certs)

questions.question: What is 'service mesh' and its relation to PKI?
options: A dedicated infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication in microservices, using mTLS (mutual TLS) for all service-to-service traffic
questions.explanation: Service meshes (Istio, Linkerd) automatically provision mTLS certificates for every microservice using an internal PKI (often SPIFFE/SPIRE). Each service gets a SVID (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Document) for mTLS authentication.
questions.takeaway: Service mesh + PKI = automatic mTLS for all service-to-service (SPIFFE/SPIRE for SVID certs)

questions.question: What is 'SPIFFE' (Secure Production Identity Framework for Everyone)?
options: An open standard for service identity in dynamic microservice environments, issuing X.509 SVIDs for workload authentication
questions.explanation: SPIFFE provides a standard for dynamic workload identity in cloud-native environments. SPIRE (SPIFFE Runtime Environment) issues X.509 SVIDs (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents) to workloads for mTLS authentication without static credentials.
questions.takeaway: SPIFFE = open standard for workload identity (SPIRE issues X.509 SVIDs for service mTLS)

questions.question: What is 'HSM-as-a-Service' (cloud HSM)?
options: Cloud-based HSM services (AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM, Google Cloud HSM) providing FIPS 140-2 L3 validated key storage without on-premises hardware
questions.explanation: Cloud HSM services (AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM) provide FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware cryptographic modules as a managed cloud service, eliminating the need for organizations to purchase and manage physical HSMs.
questions.takeaway: Cloud HSM = FIPS 140-2 L3 hardware as managed cloud service (AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM)

questions.question: What is 'hardware attestation' in TPM?
options: A process where a TPM cryptographically proves the device's hardware and software configuration hasn't been tampered with using AIK keys
questions.explanation: TPM attestation uses Attestation Identity Keys (AIKs) to generate cryptographic proof of a device's hardware configuration and boot state. Enables remote verification that a device hasn't been compromised.
questions.takeaway: TPM attestation = cryptographic proof of device hardware/boot integrity (uses AIK keys)

questions.question: What is 'Secure Boot' and how does PKI enable it?
options: A UEFI feature using certificates to verify each boot component is signed by a trusted key, preventing unauthorized OS loading
questions.explanation: Secure Boot (UEFI) uses PKI: each boot component (bootloader, kernel, drivers) must be signed by a key in the Secure Boot database (db). Unsigned or improperly signed components are rejected, preventing rootkits/bootkits.
questions.takeaway: Secure Boot = UEFI checks digital signatures on bootloaders/OS against db key database

questions.question: What is 'code signing' timestamp in software security?
options: An RFC 3161 timestamp added to code signatures, allowing the signature to remain valid even after the code signing certificate expires
questions.explanation: Countersigning code with an RFC 3161 timestamp embeds the timestamp at signing time. Even when the code signing certificate expires or is revoked, the software remains valid if the signature was made before revocation.
questions.takeaway: Code signing timestamp (RFC 3161) = signature remains valid after cert expiry/revocation

questions.question: What is an 'intermediate certificate bundle'?
options: A file (.pem, .p7b) containing intermediate CA certificates needed to complete the certificate chain from end-entity to root
questions.explanation: When a TLS server presents its certificate, it should also send the intermediate CA certificate bundle so clients can build the chain to the Root CA. Missing intermediates cause 'untrusted certificate' errors in browsers.
questions.takeaway: Intermediate bundle = intermediate CA cert file needed to complete chain (missing = browser error)

questions.question: What is 'revocation checking' hardcoded in browsers?
options: Modern browsers use CRLSets (Chrome), OneCRL (Firefox), or similar preloaded revocation data rather than live CRL/OCSP for performance
questions.explanation: Chrome uses CRLSets (preloaded compressed revocation data for important certs), Firefox uses OneCRL. Live OCSP/CRL checking is unreliable (soft-fail) and slow. CT + OCSP-must-staple are preferred modern approaches.
questions.takeaway: Chrome CRLSets, Firefox OneCRL = preloaded revocation (faster/more reliable than live OCSP/CRL)

questions.question: What is a 'Registration Authority' (RA) vs a 'Validation Authority' (VA)?
options: RA issues certificates; VA validates them
RA verifies identity for certificate enrollment; VA provides OCSP revocation status (different functions in PKI)
RA is more trusted than VA
questions.explanation: RA (Registration Authority) verifies applicant identity before forwarding approved CSRs to the CA for signing. VA (Validation Authority) provides OCSP responses for certificate revocation status checking.
questions.takeaway: RA = identity verification for enrollment; VA = OCSP revocation status responses

questions.question: What is the 'name constraints' extension in X.509?
options: A CA certificate extension restricting which domain names or IP ranges the CA is permitted to issue certificates for
questions.explanation: The Name Constraints extension in CA certificates restricts the namespaces (domains, IP ranges, email domains) for which the CA can issue certificates. Used to technically enforce that a CA only signs certs for specific organizations.
questions.takeaway: Name Constraints = limits which domains/IPs a CA can issue certificates for

questions.question: What is 'short-lived certificate' strategy?
options: Using certificates with very short validity (hours/days) to eliminate need for revocation checking — if compromised, they expire quickly
questions.explanation: Short-lived certificates (hours to days) make revocation checking unnecessary — if a key is compromised, the cert expires before it can cause significant damage. Google's internal PKI uses 6-hour validity certificates.
questions.takeaway: Short-lived certs = eliminate revocation need (compromise = wait for expiry; Google uses 6-hour certs)

questions.question: What is 'certificate transparency' for S/MIME?
options: IETF is working to extend CT to S/MIME email certificates (RFC 9549) for similar accountability as TLS CT
CT is not possible for email
questions.explanation: CT for S/MIME (RFC 9549, 2024) extends certificate transparency to email certificates, requiring them to be logged in CT-compatible logs. This improves accountability for CAs issuing S/MIME certificates.
questions.takeaway: CT for S/MIME (RFC 9549) = CT extended to email certs (similar accountability as TLS CT)

questions.question: In a hierarchical PKI, what happens if the Root CA's private key is compromised?
options: The entire PKI is compromised — all certificates issued under this root must be reissued with a new root CA
questions.explanation: The Root CA is the ultimate trust anchor. If its private key is compromised, ALL certificates in the hierarchy are untrusted — attackers can forge any certificate in the hierarchy. All must be reissued under a new root.
questions.takeaway: Root CA key compromise = entire PKI hierarchy untrusted (all certs must be reissued)

questions.question: What is the 'Subject Public Key Info' field in an X.509 certificate?
options: Contains the algorithm identifier and the certificate subject's public key value
questions.explanation: The Subject Public Key Info (SPKI) field contains: 1) The algorithm identifier (e.g., rsaEncryption OID), 2) The actual public key bits (RSA modulus and exponent, EC point, etc.). This is the key the certificate certifies.
questions.takeaway: SPKI field = algorithm identifier + subject's public key (the key the cert certifies)

questions.question: What is 'online certificate enrollment' vs 'offline enrollment'?
options: Online = CSR submitted and certificate issued via network (web enrollment, SCEP, ACME); Offline = CSR and cert transported via secure physical means
questions.explanation: Online enrollment: CSR submitted and cert returned over network (Let's Encrypt ACME, ADCS web enrollment, SCEP). Offline: CSR transferred via secure media (USB) to an offline CA for signing — used for Root/subordinate CAs.
questions.takeaway: Online enrollment = network CSR/cert exchange; Offline = secure media transfer (Root CA is offline)

questions.question: What is 'certificate authority compromise' and how is it detected?
options: A CA being hacked and issuing fraudulent certs; detected via CT logs (unexpected certs), HPKP violations, or direct audit
questions.explanation: CA compromise results in fraudulent certificates being issued. Detection methods: CT log monitoring (unexpected certs for your domains), HPKP violation reports, security researchers discovering certs, or CA's own audit/intrusion detection.
questions.takeaway: CA compromise detected via CT log monitoring (unexpected certs for your domain)

questions.question: What is 'EJBCA' in PKI?
options: Enterprise Java Beans Certificate Authority — a popular open-source, enterprise-grade CA software used for building PKI
questions.explanation: EJBCA (Enterprise JavaBeans Certificate Authority) is a widely used open-source CA software built on Java EE. It supports multiple CAs, certificate profiles, OCSP, CRL, SCEP, CMP, REST APIs, and HSM integration.
questions.takeaway: EJBCA = open-source enterprise CA software (Java EE; supports OCSP, CRL, SCEP, HSM)

questions.question: What is 'Dogtag Certificate System'?
options: Red Hat's open-source PKI software suite providing CA, OCSP, KRA (Key Recovery Authority), and TKS (Token Key Service)
questions.explanation: Dogtag (Red Hat Certificate System) is a comprehensive open-source PKI providing: CA, OCSP, Key Recovery Authority (KRA for key archival), Token Key Service (TKS for smart cards). Used in RHEL/Fedora identity management.
questions.takeaway: Dogtag = Red Hat's open-source PKI (CA, OCSP, KRA for key recovery, TKS for smart cards)

questions.question: What is 'certificate profile' in PKI?
options: A predefined configuration specifying certificate fields, extensions, validity, and key parameters for a specific certificate type
questions.explanation: Certificate profiles define templates for specific certificate types: which extensions are included (Key Usage, EKU), their criticality, validity period, allowed algorithms, and naming conventions. Different profiles for server, client, code signing, etc.
questions.takeaway: Cert profile = template for specific cert type (defines extensions, validity, key parameters)

questions.question: What is the role of 'entropy sources' in HSMs?
options: Hardware true random number generators (TRNG) in HSMs providing high-quality entropy for cryptographically secure key generation
questions.explanation: HSMs include hardware True Random Number Generators (TRNGs) based on physical processes (thermal noise, quantum effects). High-quality entropy ensures generated keys are truly random and unpredictable, critical for cryptographic strength.
questions.takeaway: HSM TRNG = hardware random number generator using physical processes (ensures truly random keys)

questions.question: What is 'key wrapping' in HSM key management?
options: Encrypting a cryptographic key with a Key Encryption Key (KEK) for secure storage or transport outside the HSM
questions.explanation: Key wrapping (AES Key Wrap, RFC 3394) encrypts a key with a KEK (Key Encryption Key) so it can be stored outside the HSM or transported securely. The wrapped key can only be unwrapped within the HSM.
questions.takeaway: Key wrapping = encrypt key with KEK (AES Key Wrap RFC 3394) for secure storage outside HSM

questions.question: What is 'certificate archival' and why is it important?
options: Preserving certificates and their private keys for long-term verification of historical digital signatures (e.g., legal documents signed years ago)
questions.explanation: Certificate archival preserves old certificates and (for encryption) private keys long-term. Legal documents may need signature verification 10+ years later. Archived certs allow verification even after expiry.
questions.takeaway: Cert archival = long-term preservation for historical signature verification (legal documents)

questions.question: What is 'key recovery' in enterprise PKI?
options: Recovering a user's private key from escrow when the user loses it, enabling access to historical encrypted data
questions.explanation: Enterprise PKI can archive users' encryption private keys (key escrow). If a user loses their key, the Key Recovery Agent (KRA) can retrieve the archived key, enabling decryption of historical encrypted files.
questions.takeaway: Key recovery = retrieve archived private key from KRA (enables decrypt of historical files)

questions.question: What is 'dual control' in PKI key management?
options: Requiring two separate people to perform sensitive key management operations (prevents single-person key abuse)
Using two keys for one operation
questions.explanation: Dual control requires two (or more) authorized individuals to cooperate for sensitive operations like: Root CA key generation, key recovery, CA backup. Prevents insider attacks by single administrators.
questions.takeaway: Dual control = two people required for sensitive key operations (prevents insider attacks)

questions.question: What is 'split knowledge' in PKI key management?
options: Knowing both public and private keys
Splitting a key into known halves
Dividing key knowledge so no single person knows the complete key (M-of-N shares via Shamir's Secret Sharing)
Sharing key knowledge with multiple CAs
questions.explanation: Split knowledge (Shamir's Secret Sharing) divides a secret into n shares where m shares are required to reconstruct it. Used for Root CA private keys: e.g., 3-of-5 scheme requires any 3 of 5 custodians to activate the key.
questions.takeaway: Split knowledge = Shamir's Secret Sharing (m-of-n shares to reconstruct Root CA key)

questions.question: What is the 'AIA' (Authority Information Access) extension in X.509?
options: An X.509 extension providing URLs for OCSP responder and issuing CA certificate download
questions.explanation: AIA extension provides: 1) OCSP URL (id-ad-ocsp) for revocation checking, 2) CA Issuers URL (id-ad-caIssuers) for downloading the issuing CA certificate (to build the chain without needing the intermediate pre-installed).
questions.takeaway: AIA extension = OCSP URL + CA Issuers URL (enables revocation check + chain building)

questions.question: What is 'path length constraint' in X.509 CA certificates?
options: A Basic Constraints extension field limiting how many intermediate CAs can exist below this CA in the certificate chain
questions.explanation: pathLenConstraint in the Basic Constraints extension specifies the maximum number of intermediate CAs below this CA. pathLenConstraint=0 means only end-entity certs can be issued below; =1 allows one more CA level.
questions.takeaway: pathLenConstraint = max intermediate CAs below this CA (0=end-entity only, 1=one more CA level)

questions.question: What does the CIA Triad in Information Security stand for?
questions.explanation: The CIA Triad is the foundational model of information security: Confidentiality (preventing unauthorized disclosure), Integrity (preventing unauthorized modification), and Availability (ensuring authorized access when needed).
questions.takeaway: CIA Triad = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability

questions.question: Which CIA Triad principle is violated when an attacker performs a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack on a web server?
questions.explanation: A DDoS attack floods a server with traffic so legitimate users cannot access it, directly attacking Availability - one of the three pillars of the CIA Triad.
questions.takeaway: DDoS attacks target Availability (the 'A' in CIA Triad)

questions.question: An attacker intercepts and reads confidential emails without modifying them. Which security goal is compromised?
questions.explanation: Reading data without authorization, even without altering it, is a breach of Confidentiality - ensuring information is accessible only to authorized parties.
questions.takeaway: Unauthorized reading of data = Confidentiality breach

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three pillars of the CIA Triad?
questions.explanation: Accountability, along with non-repudiation and authentication, is often considered an extended security concept but is not part of the original CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
questions.takeaway: CIA Triad has only 3 pillars; Accountability is an extended concept

questions.question: What is the difference between a 'threat' and a 'vulnerability' in information security?
options: A vulnerability is a potential danger; a threat is a weakness
A threat is a potential danger that exploits a vulnerability, which is a weakness in a system
A threat only applies to hardware
questions.explanation: A vulnerability is a weakness or gap in a system's defenses (e.g., unpatched software). A threat is any circumstance or event (e.g., a hacker) with the potential to exploit that vulnerability and cause harm.
questions.takeaway: Vulnerability = weakness; Threat = potential exploiter of that weakness

questions.question: What is 'risk' in the context of information security, expressed as a relationship?
options: Risk is a function of Threat, Vulnerability, and Impact/Asset value
questions.explanation: Risk is generally understood as a function of the likelihood of a threat exploiting a vulnerability and the resulting impact on an asset. Reducing any of these factors reduces overall risk.
questions.takeaway: Risk = f(Threat, Vulnerability, Impact)

questions.question: A social engineering attack where an attacker impersonates a trusted entity via email to steal credentials is called:
questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack that uses deceptive emails or messages impersonating trusted entities to trick victims into revealing sensitive information like passwords.
questions.takeaway: Phishing = deceptive emails impersonating trusted entities to steal credentials

questions.question: An attacker captures a valid authentication token and re-sends it later to gain unauthorized access. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: A replay attack occurs when an attacker intercepts valid data (like an authentication token) and retransmits it later to impersonate the legitimate user. Timestamps and nonces are common defenses.
questions.takeaway: Replay attack = capturing and resending valid data later; defend with nonces/timestamps

questions.question: Which attack involves an attacker positioning themselves between two communicating parties to intercept or alter traffic?
questions.explanation: In a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack, the attacker secretly intercepts and possibly alters communications between two parties who believe they are directly communicating with each other.
questions.takeaway: MITM attack = attacker secretly relays/alters communication between two parties

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of TLS certificate pinning in preventing which attack?
questions.explanation: Certificate pinning binds a host to a specific certificate/public key, so even a fraudulent CA-issued certificate (used in MITM attacks) will be rejected because it doesn't match the pinned value.
questions.takeaway: Certificate pinning mitigates MITM attacks using fraudulent CA-issued certificates

questions.question: Which attack technique attempts every possible key or password combination until the correct one is found?
questions.explanation: A brute-force attack systematically tries all possible combinations of keys or passwords. Its feasibility depends on key/password length and complexity; longer keys exponentially increase attack time.
questions.takeaway: Brute-force attack = exhaustive trial of all possible keys/passwords

questions.question: In cryptography, what does the 'birthday attack' exploit?
options: The probability of hash collisions based on the birthday paradox in probability theory
questions.explanation: The birthday attack exploits the birthday paradox: finding two inputs with the same hash output requires far fewer attempts (~2^(n/2) for an n-bit hash) than a brute-force preimage attack (~2^n).
questions.takeaway: Birthday attack exploits hash collision probability (~2^(n/2) attempts for n-bit hash)

questions.question: An attacker takes over an already-authenticated user's session (e.g., by stealing a session cookie) to gain unauthorized access. This is called:
questions.explanation: Session hijacking involves stealing or predicting a valid session token (e.g., a cookie) to impersonate an already-authenticated user without needing their credentials.
questions.takeaway: Session hijacking = stealing a valid session token to impersonate a logged-in user

questions.question: Malicious software that encrypts a victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key is known as:
questions.explanation: Ransomware is malware that encrypts a victim's data (often using strong symmetric/asymmetric cryptography) and demands a ransom, typically in cryptocurrency, for the decryption key.
questions.takeaway: Ransomware = malware that encrypts files and demands ransom for decryption key

questions.question: Passive interception of network traffic to capture data (such as passwords) without altering it is called:
questions.explanation: Sniffing is the passive capture of network packets, often using tools like Wireshark, to eavesdrop on unencrypted traffic and extract sensitive data such as credentials.
questions.takeaway: Sniffing = passive capture/eavesdropping of network traffic

questions.question: Falsifying the source address of network packets to impersonate another host is called:
questions.explanation: Spoofing involves forging the source identity (IP address, MAC address, email sender, etc.) of a communication to impersonate a trusted entity and bypass security checks.
questions.takeaway: Spoofing = forging source identity to impersonate a trusted entity

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a 'zero-day' vulnerability?
options: A vulnerability patched immediately upon discovery
A previously unknown vulnerability with no available patch, exploited before vendors can fix it
A vulnerability that only affects day-zero installations
questions.explanation: A zero-day vulnerability is a security flaw unknown to the vendor, meaning there are 'zero days' of protection since no patch exists yet, making it especially dangerous if exploited.
questions.takeaway: Zero-day = unknown, unpatched vulnerability actively exploitable

questions.question: Which security control category does 'encryption of data at rest' primarily fall under?
questions.explanation: Encrypting data at rest is a preventive control - it proactively stops unauthorized parties from reading data even if they gain physical or logical access to storage media, thus protecting confidentiality.
questions.takeaway: Encryption at rest = preventive control for confidentiality

questions.question: Which of the following attacks specifically targets the 'availability' component of the CIA Triad by overwhelming a system with traffic?
questions.explanation: A Denial of Service (DoS) attack overwhelms a system's resources (bandwidth, CPU, memory) so legitimate users cannot access services, directly compromising availability.
questions.takeaway: DoS attack = overwhelms resources to compromise Availability

questions.question: Manipulating a person into divulging confidential information through psychological manipulation rather than technical hacking is called:
questions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human psychology (trust, fear, urgency) rather than technical vulnerabilities to trick people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that compromise security.
questions.takeaway: Social engineering = exploiting human psychology, not technical flaws

questions.question: Which built-in Windows command-line utility is used to encrypt or decrypt files and folders using EFS?
questions.explanation: The 'cipher' command in Windows is used to display or alter the encryption of files and directories on NTFS volumes using the Encrypting File System (EFS), e.g., 'cipher /e' encrypts a folder.
questions.takeaway: cipher.exe = Windows CLI tool to encrypt/decrypt files/folders via EFS

questions.question: What underlying file system feature does Windows EFS (Encrypting File System) require?
questions.explanation: EFS is a feature of the NTFS file system that transparently encrypts individual files and folders using a combination of symmetric encryption (for data) and asymmetric encryption (to protect the symmetric key with the user's certificate).
questions.takeaway: EFS requires NTFS file system

questions.question: In Windows EFS, how is the actual file data encrypted, and how is that key protected?
options: File data is hashed, not encrypted
File data is encrypted asymmetrically; key stored in plaintext
File data uses no key at all
File data is encrypted symmetrically (FEK); the FEK is then encrypted with the user's public key
questions.explanation: EFS encrypts file contents using a symmetric File Encryption Key (FEK) for performance. The FEK itself is then encrypted with the user's RSA public key (from their EFS certificate) and stored with the file.
questions.takeaway: EFS = symmetric FEK encrypts data; FEK is encrypted with user's public key

questions.question: What is the primary difference between BitLocker and EFS in Windows?
options: BitLocker encrypts entire volumes/drives; EFS encrypts individual files/folders
EFS encrypts entire drives; BitLocker encrypts individual files
BitLocker only works on Linux; EFS only on Windows
questions.explanation: BitLocker provides full-volume/full-disk encryption protecting all data on a drive, often leveraging a TPM chip, while EFS provides file-and-folder-level encryption for individual files within a volume.
questions.takeaway: BitLocker = full-volume encryption; EFS = file/folder-level encryption

questions.question: Which hardware component does BitLocker typically use to securely store encryption keys and verify system integrity at boot?
questions.explanation: BitLocker commonly uses a TPM (Trusted Platform Module), a hardware chip that securely stores encryption keys and validates the boot process integrity before releasing the decryption key.
questions.takeaway: BitLocker typically relies on TPM for secure key storage and boot integrity checks

questions.question: In CrypTool, the Caesar cipher is classified as which type of cipher?
questions.explanation: The Caesar cipher is a monoalphabetic substitution cipher where each letter is shifted by a fixed number of positions in the alphabet. It is symmetric because the same shift value is used for both encryption and decryption.
questions.takeaway: Caesar cipher = symmetric monoalphabetic substitution cipher

questions.question: What makes the Vernam cipher (One-Time Pad) theoretically unbreakable when used correctly?
options: The key is truly random, as long as the plaintext, and used only once
questions.explanation: The Vernam cipher (One-Time Pad) achieves perfect secrecy when the key is truly random, at least as long as the plaintext, and never reused - conditions proven by Claude Shannon to make it information-theoretically unbreakable.
questions.takeaway: Vernam cipher = unbreakable only if key is random, as long as message, and used once

questions.question: In a simple XOR cipher, if C = P XOR K (Ciphertext = Plaintext XOR Key), how does a receiver decrypt C?
questions.explanation: XOR is its own inverse: since C = P XOR K, applying XOR with the same key again recovers the plaintext: P = C XOR K. This property makes XOR popular in simple stream ciphers like the Vernam cipher.
questions.takeaway: XOR cipher decryption: P = C XOR K (XOR is self-inverse)

questions.question: In classical cryptography, a 'transposition cipher' works by:
options: Using public and private key pairs
Rearranging the positions of letters in the plaintext without changing the letters themselves
Applying a hash function to the plaintext
Replacing letters with other letters or symbols
questions.explanation: Transposition ciphers rearrange (permute) the order of characters in the plaintext according to a system, without altering the actual letters used - contrasting with substitution ciphers, which replace letters.
questions.takeaway: Transposition cipher = rearranges letter order; substitution cipher = replaces letters

questions.question: Which of the following is an open-source educational tool commonly used to demonstrate symmetric and asymmetric encryption/decryption, cryptanalysis, and hash functions in a lab environment?
questions.explanation: CrypTool is a widely used free, open-source e-learning application for demonstrating cryptographic and cryptanalytic concepts, including classical ciphers, modern symmetric/asymmetric encryption, and hash functions.
questions.takeaway: CrypTool = open-source educational tool for cryptography/cryptanalysis demonstration

questions.question: Why is a monoalphabetic substitution cipher like the Caesar cipher considered cryptographically weak against modern attacks?
options: It uses asymmetric keys that can be factored
It cannot be implemented in software
It requires too much computational power
It is vulnerable to frequency analysis because letter frequency patterns are preserved
questions.explanation: Because each plaintext letter always maps to the same ciphertext letter, the statistical frequency distribution of letters in the language is preserved, allowing attackers to break it easily using frequency analysis.
questions.takeaway: Monoalphabetic ciphers are broken via frequency analysis (letter frequencies are preserved)

questions.question: When encrypting a folder graphically in Windows (right-click > Properties > Advanced > Encrypt contents), which technology is invoked in the background?
questions.explanation: The graphical 'Encrypt contents to secure data' option in folder Properties > Advanced invokes EFS, which transparently encrypts files within that folder using the logged-in user's certificate.
questions.takeaway: Graphical folder encryption in Windows = EFS under the hood

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol?
options: To allow two parties to establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel without prior shared secrets
questions.explanation: Diffie-Hellman allows two parties who have never met to jointly establish a shared secret key over a public, insecure channel, which can then be used for symmetric encryption.
questions.takeaway: Diffie-Hellman = securely establishes a shared secret key over an insecure channel

questions.question: In Diffie-Hellman, if the public parameters are prime p=23 and generator g=5, Alice picks private key a=6 and computes A = g^a mod p. What is A?
questions.explanation: 5^6 = 15625. 15625 mod 23 = 8 (since 23*679=15617, remainder 8). So Alice's public value A = 8.
questions.takeaway: Diffie-Hellman public value computed as A = g^a mod p

questions.question: What mathematical problem underlies the security of the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange?
questions.explanation: Diffie-Hellman's security relies on the computational hardness of the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP): given g, p, and g^a mod p, it is computationally infeasible to determine 'a' for sufficiently large primes.
questions.takeaway: Diffie-Hellman security = based on the Discrete Logarithm Problem

questions.question: Why is the basic (unauthenticated) Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle attacks?
options: It does not authenticate the parties exchanging public values, allowing an attacker to intercept and substitute their own values
It requires a trusted third party that can be bribed
It uses very small prime numbers by design
questions.explanation: Plain Diffie-Hellman provides no authentication of the exchanged public values. An attacker can intercept both parties' public keys and substitute their own, establishing two separate shared secrets and relaying/altering traffic - a classic MITM attack.
questions.takeaway: Plain DH lacks authentication, making it vulnerable to MITM unless combined with digital signatures/certificates

questions.question: Which real-world protocol commonly combines Diffie-Hellman with digital signatures/certificates to prevent MITM attacks?
questions.explanation: TLS handshakes commonly use DHE or ECDHE (Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman) combined with the server's certificate-based digital signature to authenticate the exchange and provide forward secrecy while avoiding MITM.
questions.takeaway: TLS uses DHE/ECDHE + certificates to authenticate Diffie-Hellman and prevent MITM

questions.question: What key advantage does Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE/ECDHE) provide over static key exchange methods?
options: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) - past sessions remain secure even if long-term keys are later compromised
questions.explanation: Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman generates a new key pair for each session, so even if a server's long-term private key is later compromised, past recorded sessions cannot be decrypted, providing Perfect Forward Secrecy.
questions.takeaway: Ephemeral DH (DHE/ECDHE) provides Perfect Forward Secrecy

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'cryptographic issue' that arises specifically from key reuse in stream ciphers like RC4?
options: Two messages XORed with the same keystream can be XORed together to cancel the key and reveal a relationship between plaintexts
questions.explanation: If the same keystream is reused for two different plaintexts (C1 XOR C2 = P1 XOR P2), the key cancels out, exposing a relationship between the plaintexts that can often be exploited via cryptanalysis to recover both messages.
questions.takeaway: Keystream reuse in stream ciphers is a critical vulnerability (key cancels out when ciphertexts are XORed)

questions.question: What was one of the earliest widely-used symmetric key exchange protocols before public-key cryptography, requiring pre-shared secret keys distributed via a trusted courier or channel?
questions.explanation: Before public-key cryptography (1976), symmetric keys had to be distributed manually or via trusted, out-of-band channels (e.g., couriers), which is impractical at scale - a problem Diffie-Hellman later solved.
questions.takeaway: Pre-1976, symmetric key distribution required trusted out-of-band channels; DH solved this scalability problem

questions.question: In the general lifecycle of a cryptographic protocol (key generation, ciphering, message exchange), what typically happens FIRST?
questions.explanation: A cryptographic protocol typically begins with key generation or key exchange (establishing the cryptographic material), followed by encryption (ciphering) of the message, and finally transmission/exchange.
questions.takeaway: Protocol flow: Key generation/exchange -> Ciphering -> Message exchange

questions.question: Which of the following historical developments directly motivated the invention of public-key (asymmetric) cryptography by Diffie and Hellman in 1976?
options: The need for faster hashing algorithms
The need to solve the secure key distribution problem inherent in symmetric cryptography
questions.explanation: Diffie and Hellman's landmark 1976 paper 'New Directions in Cryptography' was motivated by solving the key distribution problem - the difficulty of securely sharing symmetric keys between parties who have never met.
questions.takeaway: Public-key cryptography (1976) was invented to solve the symmetric key distribution problem

questions.question: How many rounds does the standard DES (Data Encryption Standard) algorithm use to encrypt a 64-bit block?
questions.explanation: DES processes a 64-bit plaintext block through 16 rounds of a Feistel network structure, using a 56-bit effective key (from a 64-bit key with 8 parity bits) to produce the ciphertext.
questions.takeaway: DES = 16 rounds, 64-bit block, 56-bit effective key

questions.question: What is the effective key length of DES after removing parity bits, and why is it now considered insecure?
options: 56 bits; too short and vulnerable to brute-force attacks with modern hardware
questions.explanation: DES uses a 56-bit effective key (64 bits minus 8 parity bits). This key space (2^56) can be brute-forced in hours with modern hardware/ASICs, making DES cryptographically insecure today.
questions.takeaway: DES = 56-bit effective key, now insecure against brute-force

questions.question: What structure does the core DES encryption algorithm use to process plaintext blocks?
questions.explanation: DES uses a Feistel network/cipher structure, which splits the block into two halves and applies a round function iteratively, allowing the same structure to be used for both encryption and decryption (with reversed key order).
questions.takeaway: DES structure = Feistel network

questions.question: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) uses which cipher structure, unlike DES's Feistel network?
questions.explanation: AES (based on Rijndael) uses a Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN) structure, involving byte substitution (SubBytes), row shifting (ShiftRows), column mixing (MixColumns), and key addition (AddRoundKey) in each round.
